We're excited to release a redesigned **JavaScript SDK** and a new approach to how we build and maintain SDKs at Nhost.

## Why we rebuilt our SDK architecture

Our previous SDK ecosystem was built around two problematic patterns. First, we maintained individual packages for each service: **nhost-graphql**, **hasura-auth-js**, and **hasura-storage-js**, with the main **nhost-js** package aggregating them. Very few people were using these individual packages, so there wasn't much benefit to maintaining them separately. Second, we had framework-specific SDKs for React, Vue, and Next.js, multiplying our maintenance burden.

This fragmented approach created several issues:

1. **Complex state management**: our SDKs relied heavily on a complex state machine that was difficult to understand, debug, and extend, leading to bugs with server-side logic and multi-tab synchronization.

2. **Maintenance overhead**: each time we added or modified service APIs (auth, storage), we had to manually implement those changes across all packages and frameworks.

3. **Inconsistency**: manual maintenance across multiple SDKs inevitably led to inconsistencies and version drift between different implementations.

The new generated SDK addresses these fundamental issues. It's dramatically simpler, the entire functions client is just 152 lines of clean, auto-generated code. The complex state machines are gone, replaced by straightforward, predictable code that eliminates the synchronization bugs and complexity that plagued the previous versions.

## Automatic SDK generation from OpenAPI specifications

Our solution was to build an **SDK generator** that automatically creates SDKs from our OpenAPI specifications. This approach consolidates everything into a single **@nhost/nhost-js** package with clean modules for auth, storage, graphql, functions, and complementary modules for things like session management or SSR support.

When we update our APIs, the SDK automatically reflects these changes - no more manual synchronization across multiple packages. The OpenAPI spec becomes the single source of truth, ensuring consistency across all implementations.

While the SDK maintains the familiar **nhost.auth**, **nhost.storage** module structure, the individual functions have been redesigned to be more explicit. Instead of a single **nhost.auth.signIn()** function that inferred the authentication method from arguments, we now have dedicated functions like **nhost.auth.signInEmailPassword()**, **nhost.auth.signInOTPEmail()**, and **nhost.auth.signInIdToken()**.

This makes the API more predictable and easier for both developers and LLMs to work with. We also now ship the OpenAPI specification directly with the SDK.

```typescript
import { createClient } from "@nhost/nhost-js";

const nhost = createClient({
  subdomain: "your-project",
  region: "eu-central-1",
});

// Clean, predictable authentication
const response = await nhost.auth.signInEmailPassword({
  email: "user@example.com",
  password: "password123",
});

// Direct GraphQL operations
const users = await nhost.graphql.request({
  query: `
    query GetUsers {
      users { id displayName email }
    }
  `,
});
```

This foundation prepares us for multi-language support. The SDK generator is designed to support multiple languages from the same OpenAPI specification. We're planning to replace the existing hand-written SDK for **Dart/Flutter** and expanding into other languages like **Golang**, **Python**, and more based on community demand.

## Transitioning from framework-specific packages

We've deprecated our framework-specific packages (**@nhost/react**, **@nhost/nextjs**, **@nhost/vue**) in favor of the new isomorphic JavaScript SDK. While this requires a bit more initial setup, the consolidation eliminates inconsistencies and version drift, while reducing maintenance burden.

The new approach works universally across all JavaScript environments: Node.js, browsers, and React Native, without framework-specific bloat. More importantly, it ensures that new features and bug fixes are immediately available everywhere, rather than requiring separate updates across multiple packages.

We recognize that this approach requires a bit more manual scaffolding for authentication flows. We're planning to release a complementary **UI library** that will provide ready-to-use auth components that work seamlessly with Next.js, Vue, Svelte, and other popular frameworks.

This approach will give us the best of both worlds: a lean isomorphic core SDK with optional framework-specific UI components for faster development.

## Getting started

The new SDK is still in beta, but we're excited to share it with you. To get started, install the SDK using your favorite package manager:

```bash
bun install @nhost/nhost-js@beta
```

```typescript
import { createClient } from "@nhost/nhost-js";

const nhost = createClient({
  subdomain: "your-project-subdomain",
  region: "eu-central-1",
});

// Authentication
await nhost.auth.signUpEmailPassword({
  email: "user@example.com",
  password: "securePassword123"
});

// Storage operations
await nhost.storage.uploadFiles({
  "file[]": [myFile],
});

// GraphQL queries
const data = await nhost.graphql.request({
  query: "query GetPosts { posts { id title } }"
});

// Functions
await nhost.functions.fetch("/my-function", {
  method: "POST",
  body: JSON.stringify({ data: "example" })
});
```

**If you're building a new app**: start with `@nhost/nhost-js` for the best developer experience and future compatibility.

**If you're using existing framework packages**: while they'll continue to work, we recommend migrating to the new SDK when convenient. Check our [migration guide](https://docs.nhost.io/sdk/migration) for detailed instructions.

**If you're interested in other languages**: our SDK generator is designed to support multiple languages, and we'll be expanding based on community interest and feedback.

This new JavaScript SDK represents progress in our commitment to providing better developer tools. By embracing automatic generation from OpenAPI specifications, we're ensuring that our SDKs stay consistent, up-to-date, and maintainable as Nhost continues to grow.

The foundation we've built extends far beyond JavaScript—it's preparing us for a future where Nhost SDKs are available in every language our community needs, all generated from the same reliable source of truth.

We're excited to see what you build with the new SDK. As always, we'd love your feedback on [Twitter](https://x.com/nhost) or [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/9V7Qb2U).
